perturbation.original_text: “Purpose”: means the exchange of Confidential Information, trade secrets, know -how and samples in order to evaluate and pursue the objective of one or more potential business arrangements with respect to Kenway’s potential to supply the Company with information, products and/or designs that are proprietary to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: “Purpose”: means the exchange of Confidential Information, proprietary data, know -how and samples in order to evaluate and pursue the objective of one or more potential business arrangements with respect to Kenway’s potential to supply the Company with information, products and/or designs that are confidential to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'trade secrets' to 'proprietary data' and 'proprietary' to 'confidential' creates an in-text contradiction. 'Trade secrets' have a specific legal meaning, whereas 'proprietary data' is vaguer. Changing 'proprietary' to 'confidential' to the Company implies all designs from the company are confidential, contradicting potential clauses that permit reverse engineering. This especially contradicts section 4.1 where it says 'All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party.' Are the designs proprietary or confidential?
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each Party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Information are granted to the other Party and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those expressly stated in this Agreemen t. Any background intellectual property rights subsisting in any information provided by either Party to the other shall remain vested in the Disclosing Party and the Disclosing Party shall grant to the Recipient a non -exclusive, worldwide, royalty free, r evocable license to use such background Intellectual Property Rights solely for the period of this Agreement and for the Purpose only. Any foreground intellectual property rights generated by CP in respect of the Purpose shall vest in CP absolutely and the Company shall acquire no interest, right or title in respect of the same.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'trade secrets' to 'proprietary data' introduces ambiguity because 'trade secrets' has a specific legal meaning, while 'proprietary data' is vaguer. More importantly, changing 'proprietary' to 'confidential' implies that all designs from the Company are confidential. This contradicts the contradicted text, which states 'All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party' and reserves all rights to it. If the designs are now 'confidential' (per the changed text), it creates uncertainty as to whether Kenway can reverse engineer or use them, conflicting with the allocation of rights in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: “Representative ”: means employees, agents, officers, advisers and other representatives of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: “Representative ”: means employees, agents, officers, advisers, subcontractors, and other representatives of the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'subcontractors' to the definition of 'Representative' creates a potential contradiction regarding liability. Section 2.2(d) states the Recipient is liable for the failure of any Representative to comply with the terms of the agreement. However, the standard liability for a subcontractor is different than that of an employee, agent or officer. This change creates ambiguity in Section 2.2(d).
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient may disclose the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to those of its Representatives who need to know this Confidential Information for the Purpose, provided that: (a) it informs its Representatives of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information before disclosure; (b) it procures that its Representatives shall, in relation to any Confidential Information disclosed to them, comply with this Agreement as if they were the Recipient and, if the Disclosing Party so requests, procure that any relevant Representative enters into a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Party on terms equivalent to those contained in this Agreement; and (c) it keeps a written record of these Representatives; and (d) it shall at all times be liable for the failure of any Representative to comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The addition of 'subcontractors' to the definition of 'Representative' creates a contradiction because Section 2.2(d) holds the Recipient liable for the failures of any Representative. The standard liability for subcontractors differs from that of employees, agents, or officers, leading to ambiguity and a potential conflict in how Section 2.2(d) should be interpreted and enforced. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Obligations of Confidentiality 2.1 The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the P urpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement;
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Obligations of Confidentiality 2.1 The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information protected and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) not use or exploit the Proprietary Information in any way except for the P urpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Protected Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement;
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Information' in (a) and 'Protected Information' in (b), while still referring to 'Confidential Information' in the overarching sentence, creates ambiguity. It's now unclear what information the Recipient is obligated to protect. This introduces a contradiction with the definition in Clause 1.1 of what Confidential Information is.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The following definitions in this Clause 1 apply in this Agreement as follows: “Business Da y” means a day (other than a Saturday, Sunday or public holiday) when banks are open for business. “Confidential Information:” means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a Party or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representatives including but not limited to: the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; any information that would be regarded as co nfidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (a) the business, affairs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans, intentions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; (b) the operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, specifications, trade secrets or software of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of 7 (c) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information; but not including any information: (a) that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the Party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless be treated as Confidential Information); (b) was available to the Recipient on a non -confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) was, is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non -confidential basis from a person who, to the Recipient's knowledge, is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Party or otherwise prohibited from disclosing the information to the Recipient; (d) was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; (e) the Parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed; and/or (f) is developed by or for the Recipient indepe ndently of the information disclosed by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text consistently refers to 'Confidential Information'. The changed text replaces this with 'Proprietary Information' and 'Protected Information' in parts (a) and (b) respectively, while still using 'Confidential Information' in the main sentence of Clause 2.1. The Contradicted Text defines 'Confidential Information' with specific details in Clause 1.1. This change introduces ambiguity because it is no longer clear if the obligations in (a) and (b) apply to the same information as defined in the contradicted Clause 1.1. The recipient now has uncertainty regarding what to protect and not disclose, creating a clear contradiction and impacting enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 The Recipient shall establish and maintain adequate security measures (including any reasonable security measures proposed by the Disclosing party from time to time) to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthori zed access or use.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 The Recipient shall establish and maintain industry-standard safeguards (including any reasonable security measures proposed by the Disclosing party from time to time) to safeguard the Confidential Data from unauthorized access or use.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'adequate security measures' to 'industry-standard safeguards' and 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' introduces ambiguity and potential for contradiction. 'Industry-standard safeguards' is vague, and 'Confidential Data' is undefined, unlike 'Confidential Information', leading to disputes about the required level of security. There are also many industries, so which industry are we talking about? This directly contradicts Section 2.1(e) which requires applying the 'same security measures and degree of care.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the P urpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly n ecessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of the Recipient; (e) apply the same security meas ures and degree of care to the Confidential Information as the Recipient applies to its own confidential information, which the Recipient warrants as providing adequate protection from unauthori zed disclosure, copying or use; and (f) keep a written record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the other in tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'adequate security measures' to 'industry-standard safeguards' introduces ambiguity, and the change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' creates an undefined term. This contradicts the contradicted section which states that the Recipient must 'apply the same security measures and degree of care', and warrants that these security measures are 'adequate protection'. The introduction of 'industry-standard safeguards' instead of 'adequate security measures' is too vague and many different industries can have different standards. Furthermore the switch to 'Confidential Data' is undefined and the change does not specify the industry, breaking the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition and thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclos ing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) save in relation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Informati on from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form ; and (c) certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflectin g, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed i ts obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclos ing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Data; (b) save in relation to its backup email systems and archives, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Informati on from its computer systems or which is stored in electronic form ; and (c) certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflectin g, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or any applicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipient to keep evidence that it has performed i ts obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' in section (a) and adding 'and archives' to the exceptions in section (b) introduces inconsistency. 'Confidential Data' is not a defined term, creating confusion about what needs to be destroyed or returned. Adding 'and archives' creates an exception to the erasure requirement, potentially allowing the Recipient to retain information indefinitely. The definition of 'Confidential Information' in 1.1 is directly contradicted. Also, the fact that it says 'Confidential Information' in the provided part (c) contradicts with 'Confidential Data' in (a)
perturbation.contradicted_text: The following definitions in this Clause 1 apply in this Agreement as follows: “Business Da y” means a day (other than a Saturday, Sunday or public holiday) when banks are open for business. “Confidential Information:” means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a Party or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representatives including but not limited to: the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; any information that would be regarded as co nfidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (a) the business, affairs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans, intentions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; (b) the operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, specifications, trade secrets or software of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of 7 (c) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information; but not including any information: (a) that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the Party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless be treated as Confidential Information); (b) was available to the Recipient on a non -confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) was, is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non -confidential basis from a person who, to the Recipient's knowledge, is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Party or otherwise prohibited from disclosing the information to the Recipient; (d) was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; (e) the Parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed; and/or (f) is developed by or for the Recipient indepe ndently of the information disclosed by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces 'Confidential Data' in section 3.1(a) instead of 'Confidential Information'. This contradicts the definition of 'Confidential Information' in Clause 1, which does not define or mention 'Confidential Data'. Furthermore, section 3.1(c) still uses 'Confidential Information' creating an inconsistency in the clause itself. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type. The addition of 'and archives' also creates a potential contradiction as it broadens the exceptions to erasure, potentially conflicting with the intent of protecting Confidential Information as defined in Clause 1.
